evidence_quotes.quote: publishers make games unplayable despite customers paying the full price for the computer games and video games, by cutting off the online servers that in many cases serve as a check the game was acquired legally.
dark patterns should certainly be called out and people should be educated and get to know some browser extensions that circumvent these practices.
one of the objectives is preventing harmful practices by influencers.
Subscriptions and their cancelling - again, if there is an alternative that doesn't require cancelling fee for a subscription, or even offers one-payment, customers can move to that one.
the contracts should be fair to the artists, so that dominant companies don't abuse their position.
inform in a prominent way about the fact that the game may expire at some point in the future

paragraphs.text: I'd like to bring attention to the fact publishers make games unplayable despite customers paying the full price for the computer games and video games, by cutting off the online servers that in many cases serve as a check the game was acquired legally. At the very least, the consumer should be informed in a prominent way about the fact that the game may expire at some point in the future, yet it flies into the face of preserving digital heritage. What is also bad is the fact that usually efforts to preserve these games by the customers can very easily and often go into grey areas of intellectual property and/or trade laws. This is also to the detriment of consumers, but caused by not having an easy way from the clash of laws and its conundrum. The customers technically have the right, but usually cannot utilize the tools legally in an easy way. So while I'm glad that there is a digital fairness act being discussed, I have some reservations about heavy handedness of the regulation and the fact some of the law is not even enforced, or enforced fairly. This is something I just want to stress, the fact some of the law is not enforced is stated in the Call for evidence document in some form. Not to mention, it's not only the typical end consumers that are at risk. Recently it has come to my attention that even some producers are falling prey to some bad practices in the digital space. The worst offending parts is the fact that breaches of existing law has already become prevalent in some cases, and dark patterns are not even part of the equation - just a pure indifference. Some high earning companies that live off streaming music don't care about rights of musicians, as far as I was able to research to the current day. There is little check whether or not the given samples of music are plagiarized, and/or made by AI but under the artist's name. A youtuber tried compiling some of the most egregious practices - more can be seen at link below. It sounds like artists have to rely on trademark law as a defense against these practices. I don't like that these streaming companies can get away with this kind of misconduct. At the same time, the regulatory overreach should not stomp platforms that could pose an alternative to these dominant platforms. It's not like stuff like this can't be prevented by some checks from these companies on their own. It's more that they don't want to lose money. So the contracts should be fair to the artists, so that dominant companies don't abuse their position. This is something that definitely should concern digital fairness act as well. Speaking of which, I believe that digital fairness should be dealt with means we know from physical world as much as possible. For instance, dark patterns should certainly be called out and people should be educated and get to know some browser extensions that circumvent these practices. Similarly, in a way there are some agencies that judge some of the goods on the market, so the customers should be informed that way. One point mentioned in the Call for evidence document is that one of the objectives is preventing harmful practices by influencers. Sometimes even the influencers become prey to the harmful practices. Reportedly, in one instance, they thought they were getting a cut from a company Honey through an affiliate link, when in fact they were being robbed by their policy. Subscriptions and their cancelling - again, if there is an alternative that doesn't require cancelling fee for a subscription, or even offers one-payment, customers can move to that one. There is one issue that concerns games, and that is the fact that it might not be advertised as a service and not being under a subscription model. This could be considered as a dark pattern or a false advertising. There is even an european initiative about the fact that publishers make these games unplayable, and it gained quite a big traction recently. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=plleJ0Zv0Ww
